reply:
*A bit of flour gets wiped onto the apron — the same motion, slower, while the thinking settles into place.*

"That question's been sitting on a lot of good minds for a long time. You're not the first to feel the catch in it."

*She turns to lean against the counter, arms folded loosely.*

"Here's how I hold it, for what it's worth. Three gods would mean three separate wills pulling three separate directions — three beings who might disagree, might work at cross-purposes, might each want their own corner of the sky. But that's not what we're told. What we're told is one will, one love, one purpose — and yet somehow... three who are genuinely distinct. Not masks the same person wears. Not three parts that together make a whole, the way a loaf is made of crust and crumb and crust again. More like — the love between them *is* God too, not a product of God. The loving and the Beloved and the Love moving between them, all the same fire."

*A pause. She's looking at the oven like she's checking it, but not really.*

"I won't pretend it wraps up clean. It doesn't. Every time someone's tried to make it tidy — said 'oh it's just one person in three costumes,' or 'oh they're really just three separate beings who get along well' — both of those cut something true off. The church kept saying *no, not quite* to both, for a few hundred years, before the words settled."

*She glances back.*

"So — three gods, no. One God who is somehow, in His own nature, not a solitary. That's what it seems to be saying. I can't see all the way to the bottom of it. But I don't think that's the same as it being wrong."
